title: The Fall and the Migration Period
bullets: On September 4, 476 the Germanic general Odoacer deposed the teenage emperor Romulus Augustulus, exiled him to a country villa on a generous pension, and informed the eastern emperor Zeno that the Western Roman Empire no longer needed an emperor.

The Germanic peoples who took over the West — Visigoths, Ostrogoths, Vandals, Franks, Burgundians, Anglo-Saxons, and Lombards — had served in Roman armies for generations and were thoroughly Romanized; the visible difference between a barbarian and a Roman by 500 was sometimes a haircut.

Clovis I, king of the Franks, accepted Catholic baptism around 496, choosing Nicene Catholicism over the Arian Christianity of his Gothic neighbors and aligning the Frankish monarchy with the entire surviving Roman religious establishment of Gaul.

Theodoric the Great ruled Italy from 493 to 526 through a Roman senatorial bureaucracy that included the philosopher Boethius and the senator Cassiodorus, then ordered Boethius's execution on charges of treason in 524.

Boethius wrote The Consolation of Philosophy in prison while awaiting execution; the book became the most widely read philosophical work of the entire Middle Ages, translated into every European vernacular including Old English by King Alfred himself.

Cassiodorus retired around 540 and founded the monastery of Vivarium with a deliberately learned program — copy classical pagan literature alongside Christian scripture — that became the model for medieval monastic learning and is one reason any Latin literature survives at all.

Bishops became the social load-bearing structure of the late-antique west: when the imperial government dissolved, the bishops did not, and the Roman administrative apparatus kept running inside the church after it stopped running outside it.

The Lombards crossed the Alps into Italy in April 568 and within four years held everything north of the Po valley, undoing most of Justinian's reconquest and producing the political map of medieval Italy.
subtitle: 476 to 568 — Germanic kingdoms, Theodoric, Clovis, the Lombards

title: Justinian's Reconquest and the Plague
bullets: Eastern emperor Justinian I sponsored three projects: the codification of Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis, the construction of Hagia Sophia, and the western reconquest — two spectacular successes and a catastrophic failure.

Tribonian's commission compiled fifteen centuries of Roman jurisprudence into the Corpus Juris Civilis between 529 and 534; the corpus would be lost in the West, rediscovered at Bologna around 1070, and become the foundation of every continental European legal system.

Belisarius destroyed the Vandal Kingdom of North Africa in a single campaigning season in 533 and recovered Italy from the Ostrogoths between 535 and 554, restoring nominal Roman rule across the western Mediterranean for the last time.

Bubonic plague arrived in Pelusium in 541 and reached Constantinople by 542, killing perhaps a quarter of the eastern Mediterranean's population in the first wave and crippling Justinian's western reconquest by killing the taxpayers, soldiers, and urban populations who would have sustained it.

The Plague of Justinian was the first of the great Eurasian bubonic plague pandemics — same disease, Yersinia pestis, that would return as the Black Death in 1347 — and it returned in waves at roughly fifteen-year intervals through the rest of the sixth and seventh centuries.
Hagia Sophia was completed in just five years and dedicated on December 27, 537; its 105-foot dome on pendentives was the largest enclosed space in the world for nearly a thousand years.

Christian missionary monks smuggled silkworm eggs out of China in hollow bamboo canes around 552, breaking the two-thousand-year Chinese sericulture monopoly and starting the Byzantine silk industry that would supply European courts with diplomatic gifts for centuries.

By 600 the Mediterranean was no longer a Roman sea and would not be again — the half-century from 540 to 590 is when late antiquity ends and the early Middle Ages begin, with bubonic plague as the trigger.
subtitle: Justinian, the Corpus Juris, the western reconquest, and the bubonic plague that ended antiquity

title: Christianizing the West
bullets: Benedict of Nursia founded Monte Cassino around 529 and wrote the Rule of Saint Benedict — a 30,000-word handbook prescribing eight hours of prayer, six of work, and four of reading per day under an elected abbot, with vows of obedience, stability, and conversion of life.

The Rule's defining quality was moderation — harder than the desert fathers, easier than the most ascetic Eastern monks — and it worked everywhere it was tried, becoming the universal Western monastic rule by the ninth century.

Without Benedict's Rule, almost nothing of Latin classical literature would exist today; the great majority of surviving Latin texts survived because Benedictine monks copied them onto fresh parchment when the originals were wearing out.

Pope Gregory the Great ruled 590 to 604 — the most consequential pope of the early medieval period — organizing famine relief in a Rome reduced to perhaps 30,000 people, asserting papal primacy over the Latin west, and dispatching the Augustine mission to convert the Anglo-Saxons in 596.

Columba founded the monastery of Iona on a small Hebridean island in 563, beginning the Celtic Christian missionary effort that would Christianize Pictish Scotland and northern England, with Aidan founding Lindisfarne off the Northumbrian coast in 635.

The Synod of Whitby in 664 forced the English church to choose between the Roman tradition of Pope Gregory's Augustine mission and the Irish tradition of Iona-Lindisfarne; King Oswiu chose Rome, aligning England permanently with continental Latin Christianity.

Bede the Venerable completed his Ecclesiastical History of the English People in 731, the first major work to use Anno Domini dating systematically — the AD/CE convention now in worldwide use — and his calendar textbook taught that the earth was a sphere, contradicting the modern flat-earth myth.

Christianization in the early medieval west was a top-down phenomenon: a king accepted baptism, his warriors followed, and over generations the population gradually adopted the new religion with old practices either banned, syncretized, or quietly tolerated.
subtitle: Benedict, Gregory the Great, the Irish missions, Bede, and the Synod of Whitby

title: Mohammed's Wave and the Iberian Loss
bullets: Tariq ibn Ziyad's Arab-Berber army of perhaps 7,000 men crossed from Morocco in April 711, destroyed the Visigothic king Roderic at the Battle of Guadalete in July, and within seven years had conquered nearly the entire Iberian peninsula.
The strait took its medieval name from Tariq's first landing point on the European side — Jabal Tariq, 'Mount Tariq' — softened in modern English to Gibraltar.

Most of Spain became al-Andalus and would remain under Muslim rule for the next eight centuries; only a thin strip of Christian kingdoms survived in the northern mountains, including Pelayo's tiny Kingdom of Asturias around the legendary skirmish at Covadonga in 722.

Charles Martel, Frankish mayor of the palace, defeated Abd al-Rahman al-Ghafiqi's Umayyad raiding force at the Battle of Tours in October 732 — a frontier raid rather than a continental invasion, but it cemented the Carolingian family's claim to military leadership of Francia.

Charles Martel's son Pippin the Short deposed the last Merovingian king Childeric III in 751 with the explicit blessing of Pope Zachary, who answered Pippin's diplomatic question with the formula that whoever holds the power should be called king.

Pippin invaded Italy in 754 and 756, defeated the Lombards, and donated the conquered central Italian territory to Pope Stephen II — creating the Papal States that would survive as a temporal sovereign entity until Italian unification absorbed them in 1870.

By 750 the western Mediterranean was bounded on three sides by Muslim power: the Maghreb, al-Andalus, and a sea increasingly dominated by Muslim raiders; western Europe was being squeezed into the northwestern corner of the former Roman world.

The Donation of Pippin permanently aligned the papacy with the Franks rather than with Constantinople, setting up the institutional model — Frankish king plus Roman pope, each legitimating the other — that would define medieval Western political order for the next thousand years.
subtitle: the Arab conquest of Spain, the Battle of Tours, and the Frankish-papal alliance that became the Carolingian project

title: Charlemagne and the Carolingian Renaissance
bullets: Charlemagne ruled the Franks from 768 to 814, conducted 53 separate military campaigns, conquered the Lombards in 774 and the Avars in 791–803, and fought a brutal 32-year Saxon War to forcibly convert the pagan Saxons of northern Germany.

The Saxon Wars included the Massacre of Verden in 782, where Charlemagne ordered the execution of 4,500 Saxon prisoners, and the Capitulary on the Saxons of 785, which made it a capital offense to refuse Christian baptism.

Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne Emperor of the Romans in St. Peter's on Christmas Day 800, the first Roman imperial title conferred in the West since 476 and the founding gesture of the medieval idea of a Christian Roman empire under papal sanction.

Aachen's octagonal Palatine Chapel, modeled directly on Justinian's San Vitale at Ravenna and consecrated in 805, still stands and contains Charlemagne's tomb — one of the few essentially intact early medieval buildings in northern Europe.

Alcuin of York joined Charlemagne's court in 781 and led the Carolingian Renaissance — a deliberate empire-wide program to standardize Latin grammar, copy classical and Christian literature, and reform liturgy and law on the basis of authoritative texts.

Carolingian monks copied around 7,000 surviving manuscripts in the ninth century alone; almost everything that survives of Cicero, Tacitus, Livy, Suetonius, Lucretius, and the Augustan poets exists today because Carolingian monasteries copied them.

Carolingian minuscule — the clean upright lowercase script developed in late-eighth-century Frankish scriptoria — was rediscovered by Italian Renaissance humanists, mistaken for ancient Roman handwriting, and turned by Renaissance typesetters into the roman type used in every Western book printed since around 1470.

The Empress Irene at Constantinople and the Carolingian court spent over a decade after the 800 coronation negotiating titles; the Byzantines eventually accepted that Charlemagne could be called emperor, but only of the Franks, not of the Romans.
subtitle: the Saxon Wars, Christmas 800, Aachen, Alcuin, and the script that became modern roman type

title: The Treaty of Verdun and the Carolingian Inheritance
bullets: Louis the Pious tried in 817 to set up a permanent inheritance scheme that would keep Charlemagne's empire intact across generations — the Ordinatio Imperii — but the birth of his fourth son Charles the Bald in 823 destroyed the plan and produced three civil wars.

Louis the German and Charles the Bald exchanged sworn oaths of mutual support against their elder brother Lothair at Strasbourg in February 842, with each swearing in the other's vernacular language so that both armies could understand.
The Strasbourg Oaths are the earliest substantial document in any language that is identifiably French rather than Latin — the moment the French language enters the historical record.

The Treaty of Verdun in August 843 divided Charlemagne's empire into West Francia (the future France), East Francia (the future Germany), and Middle Francia (a contested strip from the Low Countries through Lorraine to Italy).

Verdun is the deep structural origin of the modern political map of western Europe — the unstable Middle Frankish strip in between would be fought over by France and Germany for the next 1,100 years, ending only with the European Coal and Steel Community in 1951.

Charles the Fat briefly reunited almost the entire Carolingian inheritance in 884–887 but his humiliating settlement with Vikings at the Siege of Paris in 886 — paying 700 pounds of silver and giving them permission to ravage Burgundy — destroyed his political authority.
The Carolingian Empire as a single political entity died with Charles the Fat in early 888; the unified western European state would not be reassembled even briefly for another thousand years.

Local counts, bishops, and ambitious smaller landholders in the late ninth century began converting their public offices into private hereditary fiefs, building castles, and exercising public authority through privately controlled fortified residences — the proto-feudal transition.
subtitle: Louis the Pious, the Strasbourg Oaths, and the partition that drew the political map of Europe for the next millennium

title: The Triple Shock — Vikings, Magyars, Saracens
bullets: On June 8, 793 Northumbrian monks at Lindisfarne saw dragon-prowed wooden boats running up the beach — the conventional opening of the Viking Age, and the moment when the sea stopped being a barrier and started being a highway.

A coalition of Danish armies — the Great Heathen Army — landed in East Anglia in 865 and within a decade had destroyed three of the four major Anglo-Saxon kingdoms, leaving only Wessex under Alfred the Great as the surviving Anglo-Saxon kingdom.

Alfred won the Battle of Edington in May 878 after spending the late winter as a hunted refugee in the Somerset marshes, then forced the Viking warlord Guthrum to accept Christian baptism and partitioned England into Wessex and the Danelaw.

The English language is in a real linguistic sense an Anglo-Norse hybrid — sky, leg, knife, ill, the pronouns they/them/their, and the verb 'are' are all Norse loans from Danelaw-era settlement; the word 'law' itself is a Norse loan replacing Old English æ.

King Charles the Simple ceded the lower Seine valley to the Norse warlord Rollo in 911, on condition that Rollo accept baptism — the settlement became Normandy, and within four generations the Normans would conquer England, southern Italy, and Sicily.

Magyar horse archers from the Hungarian plain launched an estimated 33 major raiding expeditions into northern Italy, Bavaria, Saxony, Burgundy, and even northern France between 899 and 955, reaching the Atlantic coast at Provence in 924 and the Loire valley in 937.

Aghlabid Muslim raiders sailed up the Tiber in August 846 and looted the basilicas of St. Peter and St. Paul outside the Aurelian Walls; Pope Leo IV responded by enclosing the Vatican Hill in the Leonine Wall, completed in 852.

The proto-feudal response — local strongmen building motte-and-bailey castles, raising heavy cavalry, and exchanging fiefs of land for sworn military service — became the basic political architecture of western Europe for the next four centuries.
subtitle: Lindisfarne 793, the Great Heathen Army, Alfred and the Danelaw, the Magyar raids, and the proto-feudal response

title: Ottonian Recovery and the Year 1000
bullets: Henry the Fowler, Duke of Saxony, was elected king of East Francia in 919 — the first non-Frankish lineage on the throne — and used a nine-year truce with the Magyars to build fortified frontier strongholds and train his nobility in mounted warfare.

Otto I crushed a Magyar invasion of perhaps 50,000 horsemen at the Battle of Lechfeld outside Augsburg on August 10, 955; the German heavy cavalry shattered the Magyar formations, the Magyar leadership was killed or captured, and the Magyar raids on western Europe ended forever.

Pope John XII crowned Otto I Emperor in St. Peter's on February 2, 962, founding the Holy Roman Empire as a continuous institution that would survive in various forms until Napoleon dissolved it in 1806.

The abbey of Cluny was founded in 910 with a unique charter making it directly subject to the pope and free from all secular and episcopal control; under abbots like Maiolus, Odilo, and Hugh, it grew to over a thousand daughter monasteries by 1100.

Stephen I of Hungary was crowned Christian king on Christmas Day 1000 with a crown sent by Pope Sylvester II, completing the Magyar conversion to Latin Catholicism rather than to Greek Orthodoxy — and tying Hungary permanently to the Latin west.

Mieszko I of Poland accepted baptism in 966; Vladimir of Kyiv accepted Eastern Orthodox baptism in 988, married the Byzantine emperor's sister Anna, and brought the entire Rus' state into the Byzantine cultural sphere rather than the Latin one.

Four agricultural innovations — the heavy mouldboard plow, the three-field crop rotation, the rigid horse collar, and watermills — combined to more than double European food production between 800 and 1200, with European population rising from perhaps 30 million to perhaps 70 million.

By the year 1000 western Europe was, in absolute terms, smaller and poorer than the Tang-Song China and the Abbasid Caliphate — but it was on the steepest forward trajectory of any civilization on earth, and it had assembled the institutional materials for the High Middle Ages.
subtitle: Henry the Fowler, Otto I and Lechfeld 955, the Cluniac reform, the conversion of Hungary/Poland/Russia, and the threshold of the High Middle Ages
